publisher.publisher-name: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tripoli

title-group.article-title: Immunomodulatory effect of propolis Trigona spp. in mice infected with Toxoplasma gondii

contrib-group.aff: 1Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Kampus C Unair, Surabaya, Indonesia
2Division of Parasitology, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Kampus C Unair, Surabaya, Indonesia
3Doctoral Program of Veterinary Science, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Kampus C Unair, Surabaya, Indonesia
4Division of Veterinary Anatomy, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Kampus C Unair, Surabaya, Indonesia
5Institute of Tropical Disease, Universitas Airlangga, Surabaya, Indonesia

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author: Lucia Tri Suwanti. Division of Parasitology, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Universitas Airlangga, Kampus C Unair, Surabaya, Indonesia. lucia-t-s@fkh.unair.ac.id

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Toxoplasmosis is a zoonotic disease caused by Toxoplasma gondii. Infection with virulent strains of T. gondii induces excessive production of pro-inflammatory cytokines and increases fatality. Propolis has potential as an anti-inflammatory and immunomodulator.

sec.title: Aim:
sec.p: This study aimed to evaluate the pro-inflammatory cytokines response of Interleukin-2 (IL-2) and IL-17, and the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 in mice infected with T. gondii RH strain and treated with ethanol extract of Trigona bee propolis (EEP) from Lombok, West Nusa Tenggara (NTB), Indonesia.

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: A total of 36 mice were divided into six groups with six mice per group. Group 1 was considered as a negative control; mice were not infected and not treated while in groups 2 to 6, mice were infected with 100 T. gondii (tachyzoites). Mice in group 2 were infected but not treated whereas in groups 3, 4, 5, and 6, respectively, were treated orally with Cotrimoxazole 40 mg/kg body weight, EEP 1 mg, EEP 2 mg, and EEP 4 mg, the day after infection for 3 days. All mice under study were euthanized following ethical considerations with prior approvals, on the fifth day post-infection and spleens were taken to observe cytokines using flow cytometry. Statistical analysis was performed with one-way ANOVA and Duncan Multiple Range Test (you may add any statistical tool/software if used?? Like R version?, SPSS, version? If you used any of these/others)?

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: EEP (1–4 mg) increased CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD11b+ cells in infected mice, though not significantly (p > 0.05), unlike cotrimoxazole, which showed significant increases (p < 0.05). EEP at 2 mg and 4 mg (G5 and G6) reduced IL-2 and IL-17 in CD4+ T cells, and at 4 mg (G6) in CD8+ T cells. IL-10 expression increased in CD11b+ cells at 1 mg and in CD4+ T cells at 4 mg (G6).

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: Propolis ethanol extract from Trigona spp. bees acts as an immunomodulator and anti-inflammatory in T. gondii-infected mice, supporting its potential as an anti-toxoplasmosis agent.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Toxoplasma gondii is an obligate intracellular protozoan belonging to the phylum Apicomplexa, capable of infecting all warm-blooded animals (Delgado et al., 2022). The disease caused by T. gondii infection, known as toxoplasmosis, is a zoonotic condition that poses significant challenges to both public health (Aguirre et al., 2019) and animal health (Van Wormer et al., 2016). Felids are the definitive hosts, excreting oocysts in their feces, while various other animals serve as intermediate hosts. Infection occurs through the ingestion of sporulated oocysts from the environment or tissue cysts in undercooked meat, contaminated food or drink, or accidental ingestion of oocysts (Fatmawati et al., 2022). Approximately 30% of the global human population has been exposed to T. gondii, as indicated by serological evidence of anti-T. gondii antibodies (Hatam-Nahavandi et al., 2021). In immunocompromised individuals, such as AIDS patients, reactivation of T. gondii can lead to fatal toxoplasmic encephalitis (Khan et al., 2019).

The pathogenicity of toxoplasmosis is profoundly influenced by the host’s immune status and the virulence of the T. gondii strain (Sanchez and Besteiro, 2021). Both CD4+ and CD8+ T cells are integral to the host’s immune defense against T. gondii infection (El Bissati et al., 2023). Acute infection with T. gondii induces a robust release of IL-12, particularly by dendritic cells, which subsequently polarizes the immune response towards a CD4 TH1 and CD8 T cell effector phenotype (Khan et al., 2019). However, an excessive pro-inflammatory response can be detrimental to the host. Overproduction of pro-inflammatory cytokines can result in significant tissue damage, whereas an increased production of anti-inflammatory cytokines may facilitate T. gondii proliferation (Sana et al., 2022). Infection with highly virulent strains of T. gondii, such as the RH strain, elicits an overwhelming production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, leading to rapid mortality in mice (Mordue et al., 2001). Therefore, achieving a balance between Th1, Th2, and Th17 cytokine responses is critical for effective resistance to T. gondii and host survival (Sana et al., 2022).

Propolis, a natural resin produced by bees, exhibits a variety of biological activities, including antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, antiparasitic, antioxidant, and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as immunomodulatory effects (Alqarni et al., 2019; Zulhendri et al., 2022). The anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory potential of propolis has been demonstrated by its ability to decrease pro-inflammatory cytokines such as TNF-α and IL-6 while increasing the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 in various models (Alqarni et al., 2019). Egyptian propolis, for instance, has been shown to reduce TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 levels in mice infected with T. gondii (Hegazi et al., 2017). However, the specific species of bees producing this propolis was not clearly identified. Interleukin-10 (IL-10) is a crucial anti-inflammatory cytokine that helps regulate immune responses and maintain homeostasis. IL-10 inhibits the synthesis of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as IFN-γ, IL-2, and TNF-α, and plays a vital role in limiting immune responses to pathogens, thereby preventing tissue damage during infections (Couper et al., 2008).

Toxoplasmosis is a significant zoonotic disease impacting both human and animal health. The study of propolis, particularly its potential as an anti-inflammatory and immunomodulator, is crucial as it offers a promising alternative treatment for managing the disease. This research aimed to investigate the effects of propolis in experimentally infected mice models with T. gondii (RH strain) by evaluating the immune response of the anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10, the pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-2 and IL-17, treated with different concentration levels of propolis of Trigona spp. bee from Lombok, West Nusa Tenggara, Indonesia.

sec.title: Propolis samples and extraction
sec.p: Propolis from Trigona bees in Lombok, NTB, was used in this research. The bees were raised in an area with Moringa vegetation, which had been shown to produce propolis with high antioxidant activity (Tirtasari et al., 2024). Propolis extraction was performed using 70% ethanol based on a previous study (Hegazi et al., 2017). Initially, 25 g of propolis was cut into small pieces and soaked in 250 ml of 70% ethanol at room temperature for 24 hours. The extract was then filtered, evaporated at 40°C, and stored in a desiccator until it thickened. The final extract was weighed and stored for further experiments.

sec.title: Propagation of T. gondii
sec.p: The tachyzoite stage of T. gondii RH strain was obtained from the Center for Standard Testing for Veterinary Instruments (Balai Besar Pengujian Standar Instrumen Veteriner/BBPSIV) in Bogor, West Java. The propagation and harvesting of tachyzoites followed the methods described by Suwanti and Mufasirin (2018). For propagation purposes, mice were infected intraperitoneally with 103 tachyzoites, and the tachyzoites were harvested by collecting peritoneal fluid on days 3–4 post-infection or upon symptom onset. The number of tachyzoites in the peritoneal fluid was counted using a hemocytometer.

sec.title: Infection T. gondii and administration of the ethanol extract of propolis (EEP) in experimental animals
sec.p: A total of 36 mice were divided into six groups, each containing six mice. Group 1 (G1) served as the negative control; these mice were neither infected nor treated. Groups 2 to 6 (G2-G6) were infected with 102 tachyzoites of T. gondii. Mice in G2 were infected but not treated. Mice in G3, G4, G5, and G6 were treated orally with Cotrimoxazole at 40 mg/kg body weight, EEP at 1 mg/mouse, EEP at 2 mg/mouse, and EEP at 4 mg/mouse, respectively, starting the day after infection for 3 days. The mice were euthanized on the fifth-day post-infection, and their spleens were collected to examine the populations of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, CD11b+ monocytes/macrophages, and the production of cytokines IL-2, IL-17, and IL-10 using flow cytometry. Experimental data are detailed in Table 1.

Splenocytes were isolated by homogenizing the spleens and collecting the cells in centrifuge tubes. Cells were washed with PBS and centrifuged at 2,000 rpm for 5 minutes; this step was repeated twice. The splenocytes were then labeled with fluorescent antibodies (FITC or PE). Monoclonal antibodies used included anti-CD4 (FITC Clone Q4120) (Sigma-Aldrich), anti-CD4 (PE Clone Q4120) (Sigma-Aldrich), anti-CD8 (FITC Clone 53–5.8) (Biolegend), anti-CD8 (PE Clone 53–5.8) (Biolegend), anti-CD11b (FITC Clone M1/70) (Invitrogen), anti-IL-2 (PE-Cy5 Clone MQ1-17H12) (Biolegend), anti-IL-10 (PE-Cy5 Clone JES5-16E3) (eBioscience), and anti-IL-17 (PE-Cy5 Clone TC11-18H10.1) (Biolegend).

caption.title: Experimental design of mice groups treated with cotrimoxazole and ethanol extract of propolis (EEP).

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: Data were presented as cell population percentages and analyzed using one-way ANOVA with Post Hoc Duncan Multiple Range Test on SPSS 26 software.

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: The experimental protocol was approved by the Animal Care and Use Committee Universitas Airlangga No. 1.KEH.119.09.2022.

sec.title: Populations of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and CD11b+ monocytes/macrophages
sec.p: The populations of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD11b+ monocytes/macrophages in the spleen are presented in Table 2. Infected mice showed an increase in all three cell populations, with the highest increase observed in the group treated with Cotrimoxazole (G3). Treatment with EEP (1–4 mg, G4-6) in infected mice also increased the percentage of these cell types, but the increase was not statistically significant (p > 0.05) compared to the untreated infection group (G2). Ayundria and Rifa’i (2014) reported an increase in CD8+ cells but not CD4+ cells with EEP administration, indicating differences that could be due to various factors such as mouse condition, propolis origin, dosage, and duration of administration.

sec.title: Pro-inflammatory cytokines
sec.p: The expression of IL-2 and IL-17 on CD4+ and CD8+ cells are presented in Tables 3 and Table 4. Both IL-2 and IL-17 levels were elevated in the infected group (G2) compared to the uninfected control group (G1). Treatment with Cotrimoxazole (G3) and EEP (G4-6) resulted in a reduction of these pro-inflammatory cytokines relative to the untreated infected group (G2). In the groups treated with EEP, cytokine levels approached those of the control group at a dose of 2 mg for CD4+ T cells and at a dose of 4 mg for CD8+ T cells.

caption.title: Mean and standard deviation (%) of CD4+ T Cells, CD8+ T Cells, and CD11b+.

table-wrap-foot.p: Notations with the same superscript (a, b, c) on the same line indicate not significantly different (p > 0.05).

caption.title: Mean and standard deviation (%) of IL-2+ cytokine expressed by CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.

table-wrap-foot.p: Notations with the same superscript (a, b, c, d) on the same line indicate not significantly different (p > 0.05).

caption.title: Mean and standard deviation (%) of IL17+ cytokine expressed by CD4+ and CD8+ T cells.

table-wrap-foot.p: Notations with the same superscript (a, b, c) on the same line indicate not significantly different (p > 0.05).

sec.title: Anti-inflammatory cytokine
sec.p: Table 5 presents the IL-10 expression in splenocytes from CD4+ T cells and monocytes/macrophages (CD11b+ cells). In the uninfected control (G1) and untreated infection groups (G2), the percentage of IL-10-producing CD4+ and CD11b+ T cells did not differ significantly. However, the group treated with Cotrimoxazole (G3) showed a significant increase in IL-10 levels. Although the increase was less pronounced than in the Cotrimoxazole group, EEP administration beginning at a dose of 1 mg led to an increased IL-10 expression in CD11b+ cells. In the EEP treatment groups, an increase in IL-10 expression in CD4+ T cells was only observed at the highest dose of 4 mg (G6).

caption.title: Mean and standard deviation (%) of IL10+ cytokine expressed by CD4+ and CD11b+ T cells.

table-wrap-foot.p: Notations with the same superscript (a, b, c) on the same line indicate not significantly different (p > 0.05).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Toxoplasma gondii is an obligate intracellular protozoan parasite. Effective immune responses against this pathogen are largely cell-mediated (Afililla et al., 2020). The critical roles of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells in mounting immunity against T. gondii are well-documented (Takács et al., 2012). Additionally, Tussiwand et al. (2020) noted that immunity against T. gondii also involves NK cells, inflammatory monocytes, and macrophages, which produce gamma interferon (IFN-γ).

In this study, significant increases in CD4+ T cells and CD11b+ monocytes/macrophages were observed in the infected, untreated group (G2) compared to the control group (G1), although the increase in CD8+ T cells was not significant. These findings are consistent with Kugler et al. (2016), who reported increases in CD4+ T cells starting on day 5 post-infection, while CD8+ T cell increases were not significant. Similarly, Silva et al. (2014) showed elevated populations of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, with CD4+ T cells being predominant during the acute phase of infection (Dupont et al., 2014). CD8+ T cells are essential for controlling parasite invasion during both acute and chronic phases of infection (Padilla et al., 2009; Villarino and Schmidt, 2013). The observations in this study on day 5 post-infection indicate the infection was still in its acute phase.

Macrophages, which are included in CD11b+ cells (Cass et al., 2021), showed significant increases in the infected, untreated group (G2), with levels rising more than threefold (from 1.81% ± 0.46% to 7.2% ± 2.48%). Ibrahim et al. (2014) also demonstrated a threefold increase in CD11b+ cells on day 5 post-infection and a twofold increase on day 3 post-infection. CD11b+ cells produce oxygen radicals to inhibit T. gondii proliferation during acute infection (Shrestha et al., 2006) and facilitate phagocytosis (Biswas et al., 2017). Additionally, Chen et al. (2022) found that cotrimoxazole increases macrophage numbers in fungal infections among HIV patients.

Alongside increased cell populations, there were significant elevations in pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-2 and IL-17) produced by CD4+ and CD8+ cells in the infected, untreated group (G2) compared to the control group (G1). The primary mechanism for eliminating intracellular pathogens like T. gondii involves the host Th1 immune response, which produces cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-12 (Gazzinelli et al., 1991). Silva et al. (2014) suggested that CD4+ (Th17) and CD8+ (Tc17) T cells expressing IL-17 play critical roles in the inflammatory response to T. gondii. However, the increase in CD4+ T cells and CD11b+ cells in the infected group was not accompanied by an increase in IL-10, consistent with findings by Wille et al. (2001) that IL-10 is not essential for the virulence of the RH T. gondii strain.

Cotrimoxazole, a therapeutic agent for toxoplasmosis (Wei et al., 2015), significantly increased CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD11b+ cells in the treated group (G3) compared to the infected, untreated group (G2). This result contrasts with Longwe et al. (2015), who reported no significant effect of cotrimoxazole on CD4+ and CD8+ counts in HIV patients. However, our findings show that cotrimoxazole reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines and increases anti-inflammatory cytokines, corroborating its anti-inflammatory role in T. gondii infection (Bourke et al., 2019; Bourke and Prendergast, 2020).

The number of CD4+ T cells, CD8+ T cells, and CD11b+ cells in the EEP therapy group at doses of 1 mg, 2 mg, and 4 mg did not increase significantly compared to the group infected without therapy (Table 2). This indicates that EEP therapy does not significantly increase these cell populations in toxoplasmosis-infected mice. This differs from the findings by Ayundria and Rifa`I (2014), who reported increased CD8+ cells but not CD4+ cells with EEP administration. Variations in study conditions, such as mouse condition, propolis origin, dosage, and duration of administration, could explain these differences.

Despite the lack of significant increases in total cell populations, EEP therapy in toxoplasmosis mice increased the percentages of CD4+ and CD8+ cells expressing IL-2 and IL-17 and decreased CD4+ and CD11b+ cells producing IL-10 (Tables 3 and 4). This indicates that EEP can act as an anti-inflammatory agent in mice infected with T. gondii. Numerous studies have used propolis as an anti-inflammatory in various inflammatory models, including candida infections, arthritis, and ulcerative oral lichen planus, showing reductions in cytokines such as IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α, and IL-17 (Fatahinia et al., 2012; Tanaka et al., 2012; Zenouz et al., 2015). Additionally, propolis has been shown to increase IL-10 levels in several studies (Alqarni et al., 2019; Abdullkadem et al., 2022).

EEP therapy at a dose of 2 mg reduced IL-12 and IL-17 from CD4+ T cells to levels comparable to the control group, while this effect was achieved at 4 mg for CD8+ T cells. IL-10 expression on CD11b+ cells increased starting from an EEP dose of 1 mg, whereas a significant increase in CD4+ T cells occurred at a 4 mg dose. This suggests that the EEP dose affects which cell types express specific cytokines.

Overall, our findings demonstrate that EEP in T. gondii infection can increase immune cell populations (CD4+, CD8+, and CD11b+) while inhibiting pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-2 and IL-17) and enhancing anti-inflammatory cytokine (IL-10) production in vivo. Therefore, EEP holds potential as an anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory agent. The effects of EEP were dose dependent.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: EEP of Trigona spp. bees show significant anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects in mice infected with T. gondii. EEP, particularly at a dose level of 4 mg, effectively reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-2, IL-17) and increases the anti-inflammatory cytokines (IL-10). These findings suggest EEP is a promising anti-toxoplasmosis treatment through its immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. Further research should explore its potential applications in other animals.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declare that there are no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: Thank you to the Airlangga research fund for the Superior Basic Research grant with contract number 1287/UN3.1.6/PT/2023.

sec.title: Authors’ contributions
sec.p: LTS conceived and designed the proposal, coordinated the research, conducted the literature review, and wrote the article manuscripts. VN, AS, and KT were responsible for rearing the mice, collecting data, and conducting the literature review. RE wrote and reviewed the manuscript. M and HP handled the infection procedures, dissected the mice, removed the organs, and analyzed the data. SK performed flow cytometry and data analysis. All authors proofread and revised the manuscript.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: All data supporting the findings of this study are available within the manuscript and no additional data sources are required.
